sentences.text: In New York, in this part boarding house apartment, with my mother studying to be a nurse and getting her license as a private nurse and my father having made some connections in England to be a salesman of piece goods, that is, woolens, and then later he was in cottons.
And myself going to Julia Richman High School for a year.
And--
That was in 1940 to '41, yeah.
And there I was registered as Miss Gardner with a D.
I don't know why my cousins did that.
I mean, they were with a T-- whether they were afraid that you know, they might get mixed up with us.
But they recommended that I would be Miss Gardner with a D.
And the day I started high school, my mother started work in a factory that made-- well, it wasn't even costume jewelry.
It was fake jewelry, you know, putting rhinestones into something or other.
It was piecework.
And that's what she did, while she was studying to get her nurse's certificate.
And we came home that night.
And she said-- and my mother, you know, nobody-- in Germany, everybody's very formal.
And a stranger addresses you as Frau Gaertner.
And she came home, and she said, you know, it was very funny.
I got to the, to the factory.
And they called me Hannah.
And I said, that's funny.
I got to school, and they called me 'Miss Gardner.' [LAUGHING] I was all of 15.
I was 16, I guess, by that time.
And so we got used to being-- doing it the American way.
And my mother got to be a nurse.
And I got to graduate from high school the next year.
And I did very well in high school and had the choice of maybe applying to some colleges for a scholarship or getting into practical work.
And colleges that gave scholarships at that time were the seven sisters, that kind of thing.
And we felt that I probably-- I wouldn't be able to keep up socially with-- I mean, we just didn't have the money to afford the kind of things.
I mean, we had no money whatsoever.
If I wanted to-- I asked my father for a newspaper.
He said, pick up one out of a garbage can.
We don't have money for this.

So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- I went to a business school, of which there were many in those days, and learned steno and typing-- and I mean, these are now arcane skills-- and bookkeeping and stuff like that, for nine months.
I don't know how I could have spent nine months at this.
I think we were taken.
But anyway, I got a job right after that, as a secretary, and started going to college at night.
Which college was that?
Hunter College, which was-- you know, it's one of the New York City system colleges and had a very good reputation.
And went at night for two years, while I was working in one job, then another job and then decided to go during the daytime and get it over with.
Because I did my freshman year in two years.
And it would have taken-- I have a friend who did it, actually, all at night.
But I finished up in the next three years, and finished up in college and got out by 1947.
We were in contact until Pearl Harbor.
We were in constant contact.
I still have letters.
My father kept all her letters.
And I wrote to her.
And things got worse and worse, progressively.
And of course, she lost her husband, while my mother was on the boat over to this country.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think afterwards, we were able to correspond through friends and family in Switzerland.
But you know, once, once the war started, it got to be pretty impossible.
And I don't know how my father found out that she had been taken to Theresienstadt.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He certainly found out after the war, and he has documentation about it.
She and her brother and sister-in-law were taken to Theresienstadt.
And she, she died of some sort of intestinal ailment.
She was 83 at the time.
So I can't say she died before her time.
On the other hand, she had a pretty rugged constitution.
But she had had a rather, a very comfortable life.

And to envision that old lady, who was used to a very steady sort of lifestyle, on a cattle car, and-- it just, it's just unimaginable and-- and-- and the kind of end of a life she must have had, after all she had been through.
So my father never got over it.
He lived a normal life, bless him.
He died at 87.
But the fact that he was never able to save her just threw a cloud over his life.
He-- in-- in later times, he hardly ever mentioned her.
He just, it, it was too hard.
It was, you know, but I know from having gone through a lot of correspondence, that he kept every letter she ever wrote to him and lots of pictures of her and so on.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So meanwhile, he started building up a livelihood.
And because of the war, actually, things got better in this country economically.
And he was able to be a salesman.
And he was very respected as a sales-- he was very diligent and correct and you know, started making a fairly good living, I mean, So that he could go to an auction.
You know, we came here with nothing, with no-- not a stick of furniture, literally the clothes on our back, backs and what we had in the suitcases.

And so we moved out of this boarding house to an apartment in-- a small apartment in Forest Hills, which-- during the war, things were rather fluid, and you could find an apartment, because people moved to Washington-- I remember the people that we sublet from at first moved to Washington.
And they left a sofa and maybe a couple of old beds there.
And my mother, she got around.
And you know when people die, sometimes a whole household sort of gets sold for practically nothing?
Well, for $25, she picked up a bunch of household goods.
And you know, it was a lot to spend $25.
But she-- she furnished us.
And I slept on that sleep sofa in the living room until I got married.
And then we still had it, and we still got it, I guess, even into this house.
I mean, for years and years and years.
And one day-- and we used it as a, as a guest bedroom.
And one day, my father said, I'm not going to come here anymore if I have to sleep on that sofa.
It was so rotten.
So we went out.
And fortunately by that time, we were able to buy a new sofa.
Met my-- well, let me just go-- yeah, I mean we were at the end of-- of college here.
And-- and I had just taken-- and I was, and we were both in a B'Nai B'Rith youth group, which was for-- the, the parent group was for people of German origin.
So it was sort of a you know, homogeneous group.
And a youth group was set up for us teenagers and beyond.
I guess that by that time, we were in our early 20s.
And we had a little newspaper and other things that youth groups have.
And, but I hadn't really met him, until the day we all went on an outing in the Catskills.
We chartered a bus.
And I had taken the graduate record exam the day before, that same day.
I mean, I just got on the bus and was totally wiped out.
I'd never had an experience like that exam.
And, so for the first, I mean, at first, I saw nothing.
And then-- I don't know, there was some sort of a problem, which had to be discussed by a committee.
And I met him in-- in-- in this committee.
And you know, we went for a walk at night.
And, you know, that's how we met.
[LAUGHING] It was-- I remember there was a hay ride.
And we went on a hay ride together.
We barely knew each other.
[INAUDIBLE] There was not much going on in that respect.
But then we started dating.
I had dated someone else in the group before, and then I had broken that up.
And then I knew-- I had applied to Yale for graduate school.
And I got admitted.
I graduated from college and got admitted there.
And, and he would come up and every once in a while and visit me.
And so that it sort of blossomed.
And actually, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was-- well, he had not been a veteran.
So he didn't get veterans-- the GI Bill to go to college.
And he very much wanted to go to college and had been studying music.
And we were dating.
And he had his own little business.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father rather liked that business.
Neither of my parents liked the idea of his going to college and being supported by me.
But that's the way it turned out.
We-- actually, he went to Texas to study music.
He left rather abruptly at one point, because we weren't going anywhere and getting anywhere.
And so I-- but we stayed in touch.
And I went out to Aspen that summer, to the Goethe Bicentennial in 1949.
And he sent me a musical birthday greeting.
He sent me a lullaby that he had written and had it recorded by a friend in Texas.
And then he went to Utah to study.
He followed a teacher in Utah.
And so that was rather touching.
Oh, yes.
We have it recorded.
No, I can't sing.
It-- it was 'Wynken-- Wynken, Blynken, and Nod.' It was-- he set, he set 'Wynken, Blynken, and Nod' to music.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he, well, there had been another young man who was interested in me when I, I had been in California visiting my cousins.
And some years later, when I went out all that way to Utah, it was suggested that we get in touch.
And he said, where do you want to meet?
And of course, Frank was in Utah.
And so I said, well, let's meet in Salt Lake City.
And I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] don't know whether I phoned Frank.
I don't think I phoned him.
I think that I telegraphed him saying, if you want to see me, I'm getting off the bus in Salt Lake City at such and such a time.
And I almost forgot to tell this other person, the dentist, that I was coming to Salt Lake, that that's where we were meeting.
But that's-- well, at my last moment, I sent him a-- of course, he had to drive there.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we, I met-- Frank met me at the bus.
And I had told the other guy that I was coming a day or so later.
And so we went up to see where Frank was studying and quietly got engaged.
Frank had rented a car.
And in those days, you rented a car at your own risk.
And Frank barely knew how to drive.
I mean, he had learned how to drive in New York City.
He had no license.
I mean, he had a license.
He'd had trouble, trouble getting the license.
He flunked a couple of times.
Because there was no way to practice.
He didn't have a car to practice on.
And so anyway, he-- the car got stuck in, in a, in a canyon or the battery went dead or something.
So we had to be pushed out of this canyon with mountains on one side and a drop on the other.
But we made it.
And the next day, I went to Salt Lake City and met this other guy.
And he thought I was a little cool.
And he said, where do you want to go?
And I said, Grand Canyon.
So we went to Grand Canyon.
But all this time, we were very removed from each other.
And-- all he was interested in anyway was taking pictures.
And I was not very interested in that.
Anyway, that transgresses from the, from the, from the story.
And so when were you married?
We were married in September '49 and moved to New Haven, where Frank went to school.
When I had started graduate school, I picked up a catalog from the music school, and I send it to Frank.
And you know, it had all the, all the things that you had to do for the admissions.
And I said, do you think you could do this, pass the, their admissions, their exam?
And so he said, well, I can try.
And he tried and took the exams.
And we spent our honeymoon in a telephone booth, trying to find out whether he had been admitted or not.
And it turned-- because otherwise, he would have gone to Nashville.
There's-- there was a conservatory there.
And he had tried-- he, he passed through Nashville.
And it was then Deep South.
And he said, I don't want to be in the Deep South.
Just riding on the bus pained him.
And, but he would have done it.
But then when he was admitted to Yale, we moved to New Haven, right after our honeymoon.
And we got a furnished apartment with a nice Jewish family, who really sort of tried to parent us.
And, and we lived there for two years.
I was working first in a factory, as a secretary.
But because we had just gotten there, the term had already-- was imminent starting, and I just took the first thing I could find.
But the next summer, we went to be counselors in a camp.
And when we came back from that, I just beat the bushes around Yale.
And seeing that I knew the-- I had been there for a year, I knew where to go.
And found a job in the political science department, with the Center of International-- it was then the Institute of International Studies.

In fact, it turned out that my boss who hired me as a research assistant and a secretary, if necessary-- when I told my mother this, she said, I told you two years ago, when you started going to Yale, to look up this man.
His wife is related, you know-- six degrees of separation-- to us.
And-- and you should have looked him up then.
And of course, children never do what their parents tell them to do.
And so I hadn't looked him up.
But we got to be very good friends.
And next week is his 85th birthday.
I must write him.
He-- and he's still alive and active and--
Until I retired.
[LAUGHS] The story is that the Center of International-- the Institute of International Studies got into some sort of a disagreement with the Yale administration and were asked to leave.
And so the whole-- I mean, it decimated the political science department.
But about-- more than a dozen professors left.
And this man, Professor Gabriel Almond, one day called me into his office and said, we're all moving to Princeton.
Do you want to come along?
Boom!
And it was the year that Frank graduated from music school.
And we knew that in order for him to work, we had to be in commuting distance from New York.
And he didn't want to particularly be on Long Island.
And so you know, this seemed to be a nice area.
So we had one day to look around.
You know, we drove to, to, to Princeton.
And we couldn't find anything in Princeton that we could afford to rent.
So somebody recommended something in Hopewell.
And we started looking around.
And one thing led to another.
And he said, this is too far from the railroad.
I can't commute from here.
Well, it turned out that at the time, there was a-- the Reading Railroad went right through Hopewell.
And it had a couple of commuter trains a day.
And so we found what was a very nice airy apartment over three garages, in a rather peculiar place in Hopewell and lived there happily for five years.
Even though my mother took one look at it and said, well, maybe six weeks, until you find something else.
I was working at Princeton University.
And--
As a research assistant, for the same man that I had worked at Yale.
And gradually, the Yale people sort of dispersed.
I was the last who was still there.
And, and I worked there until Naomi was born, our oldest.
Three weeks until before Naomi was born.
She was born on January 24, 1953.
And I quit as of December 31.
And-- and then Eva was born there, too, in February, on February 27, 1955.
And, but by that time, I really wanted to live in Princeton.
And by that time, Frank had gotten a job.
And we felt that we were able to swing a house, with my parents' help, I must say.
And so we started looking around for, for-- you know, we looked in these various developments that were even then springing up, all over Princeton.
And we found this house.
And we moved here in 1956.
And we've enlarged it three times since then, or changed it around.
And, and Miriam was born in 1958, on January 6.
And, and we've lived here, happily ever after.
I retire-- oh, well, then I, I quit when Naomi was born.
And I picked it up again when Miriam was in first grade, which was 12 years later.
And, and I was still his research assistant when I started.
But the job of editor of the journal, opened up.
In fact, it opened up several times.
And each time, I said, I don't want to be stuck with deadlines when I have children at home.
And so by the time Miriam went to school, I was able to, to accept this job, you know.
And it became full time quite quickly.
And, and so I-- that was in '50.
Well, no, when she was six, she started school, in the early '60s.
And I started working as editor of this journal, and retired in 1990.
And, and it was-- and immediately, I mean I-- I knew what I wanted to do when I retired.
It was something I'd been wanting to do for a long time, namely be a docent at the Art Museum of Princeton University.
And I applied even before my retirement date, saying, I'm going to retire and so on.
So I, I never even missed a week.
I you know, jumped right from one thing into the other.
And I'm very happy with the, with my retirement project, which is a docent at the art museum.
I continue at that-- in that position, yes.
We have grandchildren.
Yes.
Eva married Alan Radding in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1981.
And, and she was at the-- in Boston at the time, going to-- taking a graduate degree at Boston University.
And she, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they got married here in Princeton.
And they moved to Brookline.
And then, and now they live in Newton Highlands.
And the children, our granddaughters-- Lisa is, has just turned 10.
And Amy is going to turn seven in a couple of weeks.
And they're-- what can I tell you?
They are our grandchildren.
[LAUGHS] Tell me about some of the positions, some of the projects that you've, that you've been involved with, in addition to your position.
You've held--
Oh, I've, when the children-- when I was home, being just the mother, I felt I should do some volunteer work.
And I, of course my connection with B'Nai B'Rith goes way back.
So I, when we-- when we founded the local chapter of B'Nai B'Rith Women, I was one of the charter members.
And after about two, three years, I, I served as president for a year.
And also the, the women's division of the Jewish center, I was chairman for a year, during the time when the children were small.
And I had nothing to do but be on the telephone.
[LAUGHS] And that was you know, very satisfying to be able to give some service.
And I've-- I'm also interested in, in AFS, the American Field Service.
Because Miriam was in France for a year there, as an exchange student, had a very happy experience.
And so I do a little bit for them.
And--
Yes, yes.
You don't forget that.
You-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, when I see poetry-- I have a poem that I clipped from the that every once in a while, I look at it.
And that's what it's about.
And yes, it-- it-- it stays as a special interest.
It, it is not-- it is, the subject of the Holocaust, and even of Germany as-- as such and of Mainz is a-- is one that is, that remains very special.
It, it's part of me.
And in fact, it's special to the children, too.
And I think, I hope it will be to the grandchildren.
It's something they have to know about.
They have to know what it was like before and what it was like during.
And you know, they read about Anne Frank.
Well, it could have been me.
And I want them to know that.
